metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify and commit to ONE reconciler signing-seam implementation approach: extract a signing-only helper from `_apply_authorship` (operating on an already-composed event dict, no commit side-effect) and call it from `_write_event_file` after stamping `author_id` — and document why routing through `append_event` is ruled out (its commit side-effect is incompatible with the reconciler's deferred-commit model).
findings.finding: The plan leaves the reconciler signing-seam fix ambiguous ('routed through it OR stamp+sign inline') without choosing one approach or providing positive rationale for either. Routing through `append_event` is actually mechanically incompatible with the reconciler's architecture: `append_event` calls `write_and_push` which immediately commits and pushes, whereas `_write_event_file` intentionally writes files without committing (the reconciler's own orchestration commits later). An implementer who chooses the 'route through seam' option would corrupt the reconciler's deferred-commit model.
findings.location: Workstreams section / 'Authenticated-identity rollout' subsection: 'the reconciler's inbound writer _write_event_file … bypasses it today and must be routed through it (or stamp+sign author fields inline)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extract a `_sign_event_inplace(event: dict, signing_key: str, author_id: str) -> None` helper from `_apply_authorship` in `_seam.py` that performs only the DSSE signing and `event['author_sig']` stamp with no config reads or commit side-effects. Call it from `_write_event_file` after stamping `author_id` from the bot identity env var. Add a one-line comment in `append_event` noting that `_write_event_file` uses the extracted helper (not the full seam) to keep the commit model consistent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the OR-choice in the reconciler fix: specify definitively whether `_write_event_file` is refactored to route through `_seam.append_event` (requiring `repo_root` threading into all 12+ call-sites) or to stamp `attribution_fields` + sign inline (requiring a self-contained signing helper in `inbound_translate.py`).
findings.finding: The reconciler fix is specified as an unresolved OR-choice ('route through it (or stamp+sign author fields inline)'), leaving the implementation design decision entirely to the executor. These two approaches have fundamentally different call-site impacts: routing through `_seam.append_event` requires threading `repo_root` and a `tracker: Path` through all callers of `_write_event_file` (confirmed: 12+ call-sites in `apply_inbound.py` and `apply_inbound_records.py`), while stamping inline requires re-implementing `attribution_fields` + sshsig signing logic inside `inbound_translate.py`. This is a processing protocol decision rule that must be resolved before implementation.
findings.location: Workstreams section — 'Authenticated-identity rollout (setup only)': '…the reconciler's inbound writer `_write_event_file` … bypasses it today and must be routed through it (or stamp+sign author fields inline)'
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer the inline-stamp approach to avoid disrupting the reconciler's commit orchestration: add a new helper `_stamp_attribution(event, repo_root)` in `inbound_translate.py` that calls `_seam.attribution_fields` and `_seam._apply_authorship`, and call it from `_write_event_file` after the event dict is built. This isolates the change to a single function and avoids signature churn at 12+ call-sites.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove 'signature mirror' from 5566's 'record the KEEP decisions' instruction; update to reference only completion_floor_active as the kept/activated item, and ensure the signature mirror retirement is recorded (not kept) in 5566's sweep.
findings.finding: Child 5566 contains a conflicting assumption about the signature mirror: its What section says 'record the KEEP decisions (signature mirror, completion floor) where docs discuss them,' but the signature mirror is being RETIRED (not kept) by sibling 7ed9. This contradicts 7ed9's scope and will produce incorrect documentation if 5566's instruction is followed literally.
findings.location: child 5566-dac3-5b7f-49ae — What section, final paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: In 5566's What section, change 'record the KEEP decisions (signature mirror, completion floor)' to 'record the KEEP/activation decision for completion_floor_active; for the signature mirror, confirm 7ed9's retirement entry in docs/migrations.md is present and no stale KEEP reference exists.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reframe the 'can sign' success criterion to match the actual in-scope deliverable: e.g., 'the code path is wired to the signing seam for every writer environment, key-delivery configs/scripts are committed, and operator provisioning steps are documented — verified in-session via code review and the test-suite guard; live signing in AWS/GHA is an operator-attested post-provisioning check.'
findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'Every ticket-event-writing environment can sign as its intended identity (bot for AWS/GitHub)' is not in-session completable for the AWS and GitHub environments because actual secret provisioning is explicitly deferred to an operator/escalation step, making end-to-end signing verification post-deployment.
findings.location: Success Criteria section, third bullet: 'Every ticket-event-writing environment can sign as its intended identity (bot for AWS/GitHub) via the per-environment key delivery above'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Every ticket-event-writing environment can sign as its intended identity (bot for AWS/GitHub)' with 'The signing seam is wired for every ticket-event writer (local, AWS auto-lander/review-bot, GHA reconciler + canary), per-environment key-delivery configs/scripts are committed, and operator provisioning steps are documented in-repo; [operator-attested] live signing in AWS and GHA environments is verified after secret provisioning.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the artifact in which operator provisioning steps are recorded (e.g., 'documented in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` as a commented TODO block AND in `docs/ops/signing-key-provisioning.md`') so the AC is deterministically evaluable.
findings.finding: The last AC bullet ('actual secret provisioning… is recorded as an operator step where it cannot be completed in-repo') does not specify WHERE the operator step is recorded, leaving the done-state artifact undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, last bullet: 'actual secret provisioning that requires AWS/GitHub access is recorded as an operator step where it cannot be completed in-repo'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC bullet: 'documented as a commented TODO block in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` (for AWS) and in the GHA workflow YAML (for GitHub Actions), each citing the SSM parameter name / secret name and the 0600 file path to write it to.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the reconciler implementation choice: state whether the fix MUST route through `_seam.append_event` / `_apply_authorship` (preferred — keeps the signing seam canonical) or whether inline stamping is acceptable, so the executor does not have to choose.
findings.finding: The reconciler fix description offers an unresolved OR choice — 'route through it (or stamp+sign author fields inline)' — deferring a real implementation design decision to the executor without criteria for which approach to take.
findings.location: Workstreams section, Authenticated-identity rollout bullet: 'the reconciler's inbound writer _write_event_file … must be routed through it (or stamp+sign author fields inline)'
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the parenthetical alternative and commit to the primary path: 'must be routed through `_seam.append_event` / `_apply_authorship`; inline stamping is not acceptable as it would bypass the canonical signing seam.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the AWS-side signing-key materialization script (e.g. `infra/gerrit/materialize-signing-key.sh`), specify its integration point in `compose-up.sh` or `user_data.sh`, and explicitly require fail-closed behavior (exit non-zero on empty/None/CHANGEME SSM value), consistent with `materialize-deploy-key.sh` and `materialize-g2p-config.sh`.
findings.finding: The AWS-side signing-key materialization script — responsible for writing `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` from SSM to `/run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing-key` (0600) — is described in prose but left unspecified as an IaC/boot-script deliverable: no script name, no integration point in `compose-up.sh` or `user_data.sh`, and no explicit fail-closed posture. Every existing materialization script in the repo (`materialize-deploy-key.sh`, `materialize-g2p-config.sh`) is explicitly fail-closed (exits non-zero on empty/None/CHANGEME). Without specifying this, the new script could be implemented fail-open, leaving the signing identity silently absent rather than halting the deploy.
findings.location: Secret delivery mechanism section (AWS review-bot + auto-lander paragraph); infra/terraform/ssm.tf (rebar_secret_params list)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a story-level deliverable: 'Create `infra/gerrit/materialize-signing-key.sh` (mirroring `materialize-deploy-key.sh`) that reads `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` from SSM, writes it to `/run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing-key` (0600), and exits 1 if the value is empty/None/CHANGEME. Invoke it from `compose-up.sh` before `docker compose up` (after `materialize-g2p-config.sh`). Add `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` to `rebar_secret_params` in `ssm.tf`.' Mark actual secret provisioning in AWS SSM as an operator step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the signing-seam-as-mandatory-contract decision in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update: resolve the 'route through seam vs. stamp+sign inline' choice to a single canonical pattern and document it so all current and future event-writing paths implement it consistently.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new cross-cutting architectural constraint — all ticket-event writers MUST route through the signing seam (`_seam.append_event` / `_apply_authorship`) — but does not specify capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc. The implementation is also left partially open ('or stamp+sign author fields inline'), meaning future contributors lack a single authoritative record of the chosen pattern.
findings.location: Workstreams / Authenticated-identity rollout section; Success Criteria bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to the plan (or a referenced ADR file) that: (1) resolves 'route through `_seam.append_event`' as the canonical approach (not inline stamping), (2) states that any new event-writing path MUST use the seam, and (3) points to a CLAUDE.md or `docs/architecture/signing-seam.md` entry capturing this invariant.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism for the test-suite defensive guard against a stray `REBAR_IDENTITY_REQUIRE_AUTHENTICATED` env: name whether it is an autouse conftest fixture (like the existing `_gate_source_local_by_default` and `_compaction_horizon_zero_by_default` patterns), which test tiers it covers, and whether it unconditionally unsets the var or only when not set by the test itself.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria references 'a defensive guard so a stray global enforcement env can't red the build' as a required deliverable, but no protocol for this guard is specified anywhere in the plan — not its mechanism (conftest autouse fixture? pytest.ini `env` setting? per-test monkeypatch?), scope (unit tier only? all tiers?), or acceptance signal. The existing `tests/conftest.py` does not autouse-unset `REBAR_IDENTITY_REQUIRE_AUTHENTICATED` (confirmed by reading conftest.py in full). This is a decision rule referenced without co-location with the test infrastructure schema.
findings.location: Success Criteria section — 'the test suite has a defensive guard so a stray global enforcement env can't red the build'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an autouse fixture to `tests/conftest.py` following the existing `_gate_source_local_by_default` pattern: `if 'REBAR_IDENTITY_REQUIRE_AUTHENTICATED' not in os.environ: monkeypatch.setenv('REBAR_IDENTITY_REQUIRE_AUTHENTICATED', '0')`. Document in the plan that this fixture is the guard and name it explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle the epic (or add a prominent disclaimer) to accurately reflect the scope — e.g., 'authenticated-identity signing infrastructure setup' — and ensure the deferred flag-flip is captured as an explicit follow-on deliverable with a named owner/ticket.
findings.finding: The plan title promises 'authenticated-identity enablement' but the body explicitly scopes the `require_authenticated` flag flip as OUT OF SCOPE, delivering signing-infrastructure setup only — the authentication feature is not enabled by this plan.
findings.location: Title; Workstreams / 'Authenticated-identity rollout (setup only)' section
findings.suggested_fix: Change the title to 'Prune settled/stale config values + authenticated-identity signing setup + doc-accuracy sweep' and add an AC line: 'A follow-on ticket for the require_authenticated flag flip is created and linked.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before removing `emit_legacy_signature_mirror` from the schema, assert in the plan what the rollback path becomes post-removal (e.g. compensating-forward: re-add the key in a follow-on PR if a pre-attestations reader is discovered), or retain the key as a deprecated no-op for one release cycle with a `_ALIASES`-style deprecation warning rather than a hard removal, so the config toggle remains available without a code deploy.
findings.finding: Removing `compact.emit_legacy_signature_mirror` eliminates the only documented, no-code-change rollback path for the snapshot compiled_state format change (legacy `signature` key omission from new snapshots), without asserting the post-removal rollback state or providing a substitute rollback mechanism.
findings.location: Workstreams section: 'compact.emit_legacy_signature_mirror (a one-way door — landed behind a readiness-gate check)'; Success Criteria bullet 1; docs/migrations.md 'Rollback (AC2)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `trap 'rm -f "$keyfile"' EXIT` (or equivalent) to the GHA signing-key materialization step so the private key file is deleted when the step/job exits, and declare whether self-hosted runners are in or out of scope for this delivery path.
findings.finding: The GitHub Actions signing-key materialization step specifies `mktemp` + `chmod 0600` but does not specify a cleanup trap (`trap 'rm -f "$keyfile"' EXIT`). On self-hosted runners (which the plan does not scope out), the private signing key persists on disk between job runs, constituting a residual credential exposure on the boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Secret delivery mechanism — 'GitHub Actions reconciler + canary' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix:
In the workflow snippet for the GHA path, replace the bare mktemp pattern with:
```bash
keyfile=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "$keyfile"' EXIT
chmod 0600 "$keyfile"
printf '%s' "$REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY" > "$keyfile"
export REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY="$keyfile"
```
Also add a plan note: 'This delivery path is scoped to GitHub-hosted ephemeral runners; self-hosted runners require additional filesystem cleanup guarantees.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `emit_legacy_signature_mirror` to 5566's repo-wide grep AC so all seven removed keys are covered.
findings.finding: Child 5566 (doc-accuracy sweep) omits `emit_legacy_signature_mirror` from its repo-wide grep acceptance criterion, leaving the parent AC-D ('no committed doc/help/release-note references any removed config key') partially uncovered for one of the seven approved removals.
findings.location: child 5566-dac3-5b7f-49ae — Acceptance Criteria, first bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In 5566's first AC bullet, extend the key list to include `emit_legacy_signature_mirror` and add its known locations (docs/config.md compact section, docs/migrations.md if 7ed9 leaves any residual toggle reference) to the sweep.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC item to 297d requiring that each environment's signing-key delivery path is documented (per the parent's 'Secret delivery mechanism' section) and that operator-only provisioning steps (AWS SSM creation, GitHub Actions secret upload) are recorded as named operator steps.
findings.finding: Child 297d's acceptance criteria commit to signed events being produced ('each environment produces SIGNED events') but do not commit to the documentation deliverable the parent AC-E requires: a named, documented signing-key delivery path per environment plus operator steps recorded where in-repo provisioning is impossible. This is a scope-narrowing relative to the parent criterion.
findings.location: child 297d-a233-ce1b-4484 — Acceptance Criteria, item 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 297d's AC: '- [ ] Each environment's signing-key delivery path is documented in a committed doc or README section, and operator-only provisioning steps (SSM SecureString creation, GitHub Actions secret upload) are explicitly recorded as out-of-repo operator steps per the parent plan's Secret delivery mechanism section.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies code-level removal: e.g. 'A grep of `src/` for each removed key name (as a Python identifier or string literal) returns zero hits, confirming no dead conditional branch remains.'
findings.finding: Success Criteria bullet 1 requires that removed keys have 'their behavior hardcoded always-on (no dead back-out branch remains),' but no Acceptance Criterion verifies the code hardcoding — AC[2] only checks that the key name is absent from rebar.toml and produces no 'unknown key' warnings, and AC[4] only checks docs. A removal that silently stubs the behavior (e.g. leaves a flag-gated branch defaulting to the old path) would pass all ACs but violate the stated success criterion.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 1 vs AC[2] / AC[4]
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] `grep -r "baseline_dual_write\|baseline_consumer_swap\|progressive_drift_refresh\|remediation_mode\|novelty_drop_active\|require_signature_for_close\|emit_legacy_signature_mirror" src/` returns zero hits.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering the `completion_floor_active` monitoring note, e.g. 'The `completion_floor_active` activation story includes (or links to) a Monitoring section documenting the `dropped[]` / `drop_reason="completion"` sidecar audit surface.'
findings.finding: The Success Criteria for `completion_floor_active` includes a sub-deliverable — 'a documented Monitoring note naming the sidecar `dropped[]` / `drop_reason="completion"` audit surface' — that has no covering Acceptance Criterion. Every other deliverable in the plan has at least one explicit AC; this monitoring-note requirement is orphaned.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 2 vs Acceptance Criteria section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback path for `completion_floor_active` (e.g., document that setting `verify.completion_floor_active = false` in `rebar.toml` is the immediate revert, and that the dropped[] audit surface should be monitored for N days post-enable before the rollback window closes).
findings.finding: The `completion_floor_active` enablement is a live runtime behavior change (it will begin dropping events with drop_reason='completion' in all environments) delivered as a single config flip in rebar.toml, with no staged rollout path, no canary, and no explicit cheap rollback mechanism described beyond editing rebar.toml (which is a code change, not an operational revert).
findings.location: Workstreams / 'Activate by dogfooding' section; Success Criteria bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' note to the dogfooding AC: 'If unexpected drops appear (monitor dropped[] for 7 days), revert by setting verify.completion_floor_active = false in rebar.toml and opening a follow-up calibration issue.' This makes the rollback path explicit and cheap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 7ed9 to 5566's explicit dependency list so 5566 is sequenced after all seven removal stories have landed.
findings.finding: Child 5566 does not list 7ed9 as an explicit dependency, creating an ordering gap: if 5566 closes before 7ed9 lands, docs/migrations.md and any other emit_legacy_signature_mirror references will not yet exist in their final state when 5566 sweeps, making the sweep incomplete and the parent AC-D unverifiable at 5566's close time.
findings.location: child 5566-dac3-5b7f-49ae — Scope section ('Depends on stories d6bd, 4cdf, and the require_signature retirement having landed')
findings.suggested_fix: In 5566's Scope section, change the dependency statement to: 'Depends on stories d6bd, 4cdf, 28f1, and 7ed9 having landed so the final state is swept.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that the AWS `fetch-secrets.sh` path will 'materialize [the signing key] to a 0600 file in a dedicated path' and cross-references 'matching the GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN / ANTHROPIC_API_KEY pattern (ADR-0008)' — but the existing `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` has no mechanism for writing a standalone 0600 key file: it writes ALL secrets as `KEY=VALUE` lines into a single `.env` file (lines 99–116). SSH private keys are multi-line PEM blocks that cannot be set as a `.env` value and then used as a `key_path` argument to `sshsig.sign()`. The referenced `GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN`/`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` pattern is single-line tokens into env vars — an SSH private key requires a different materialization pattern. The plan has not verified this capability claim against the actual `fetch-secrets.sh` implementation.
findings.location: Secret delivery mechanism section — 'AWS review-bot + auto-lander' bullet; infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh lines 99–116
findings.suggested_fix: Add a paragraph to the AWS delivery bullet explaining the required fetch-secrets.sh extension: after fetching the SSM parameter into a shell var (e.g. `rebar_bot_signing_key`), write it to a dedicated file (e.g. `/run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing-key`, mode 0600) rather than a .env line, then export `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY=/run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing-key` in the .env. This mirrors the `g2p-github-pat` → `infra/gerrit/materialize-g2p-config.sh` pattern (infra/terraform/ssm.tf lines 33–41), which already demonstrates the 'materialize to file rather than .env' precedent.

findings.finding: The plan says `fetch-secrets.sh` 'materializes [the new `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` SSM parameter] to a 0600 file in a dedicated path' for the AWS review-bot/auto-lander containers, but neither the plan's delivery checklist nor its AC explicitly requires editing `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`. The current script has no entry for this parameter, so the SSM slot will exist after the Terraform apply but will never be read or written to disk at container boot — leaving the containers without the signing key despite it being provisioned.
findings.location: Secret delivery mechanism section (AWS review-bot + auto-lander bullet); `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh`; AC line 5
findings.suggested_fix:
In `fetch-secrets.sh`, after the existing `get_param` calls, add:
```bash
rebar_bot_signing_key="$(get_param_optional rebar-bot-signing-key)"
```
Then in the file-write block, if non-empty, write the key to a dedicated path (e.g. `/run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing-key`, `chmod 0600`) and emit `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY=/run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing-key` into the `.env`. Add this edit to the plan's delivery checklist and to AC5.

findings.finding: The plan's AWS secret delivery asserts that fetch-secrets.sh 'materializes it to a `0600` file in a dedicated path', but the real fetch-secrets.sh outputs all secrets into a KEY=value .env file (infra/compose/.env), not to an individual raw file on disk. Key-to-file materialization for signing keys is a distinct mechanism in this codebase (e.g. materialize-deploy-key.sh, materialize-g2p-config.sh). The plan conflates the two patterns, so the implementation will need either a new materialize-signing-key.sh script or a different delivery mechanism (e.g. writing REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY= into the .env and having the container write-to-tmpfile on startup).
findings.location: Secret delivery mechanism (per environment) — AWS review-bot + auto-lander bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `infra/gerrit/materialize-signing-key.sh` script (mirroring `materialize-deploy-key.sh`): read `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` from SSM, write the raw key bytes to a dedicated 0600 path (e.g. `/run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing-key`), then set `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` to that path in the container environment. Wire it into `compose-up.sh` before the containers start, same as `materialize-g2p-config.sh`.

findings.finding: Child 297d's AWS delivery mechanism for the signing key conflicts with the actual architecture of `fetch-secrets.sh`. The parent plan (and child 297d) states that `fetch-secrets.sh` materializes the SSM param `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` 'to a `0600` file in a dedicated path', which containers then point `identity.signing_key` to. But the real `fetch-secrets.sh` writes a single KEY=value `.env` file (not a raw key file at a dedicated path). The signing seam needs a FILE PATH pointing to a raw OpenSSH private key (`sshsig.sign(key_path=...)`), not an env-var containing the key material. The delivery mechanism described does not match the existing script's behavior and would require `fetch-secrets.sh` to be extended to write raw key content to a dedicated file.
findings.location: child 297d-a233-ce1b-4484 — What sub-task 3 (AWS review-bot + auto-lander); parent plan 'Secret delivery mechanism' section
findings.suggested_fix: Update child 297d sub-task 3 to explicitly scope modifying fetch-secrets.sh to write the raw private key to a dedicated 0600 file (e.g. /run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing-key) and set REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY to that path — rather than emitting it as a KEY=value env line.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
